name: Pacific Rim Whale Festival
description: A week-long celebration of the annual gray whale migration through the waters off Tofino and Ucluelet. Events include the Parade of Whales and Wonders, the Chowder Chowdown, Maritime Kids Days, and the Baleen Bash closing party. The gray whales pass close to shore — around 20,000 migrate north from Baja to Alaska each spring.
location: Tofino, Ucluelet, and Pacific Rim National Park Reserve

name: Tofino Shorebird Festival
description: Every spring, thousands of sandpipers, plovers, whimbrels, and 40+ other shorebird species pass through Tofino's mudflats and beaches on their migration from Central America to Arctic breeding grounds. The festival offers guided walks, birding paddles through Lemmens Inlet's tidal flats, and expert-led talks. One of BC's largest whimbrel concentrations is here.

timing.approximate_dates: Late April through early May (April 27 – May 1, 2026)

location: Tofino mudflats, Lemmens Inlet, Pacific Rim National Park Reserve

name: Storm Watching Season
description: Tofino receives nearly 5 meters of annual rainfall, and the bulk of it arrives in spectacular fashion between November and February. Fifteen to twenty mega-storms per season, with winds reaching hurricane force, crashing waves, and horizontal rain. Hotels like the Wickaninnish Inn were built for this — massive windows, hot tubs, and front-row seats to the Pacific's raw power.

timing.approximate_dates: October through March; peak storms December–February

location: Tofino and Ucluelet beaches — Cox Bay, Chesterman Beach, Long Beach, Wickaninnish Beach

name: Tofino Wine & Dine Festival
description: A two-day outdoor festival on the beachfront at Tin Wis Resort, celebrating BC's culinary and beverage scene. Up to 100 restaurants, wineries, distillers, breweries, and artisans. Two themed days — west coast seafood and wine on one day, smoked meats, spirits, and cocktails on the next. Live music throughout. Intimate and coastal.
location: Best Western Plus Tin Wis Resort, Mackenzie Beach, Tofino

name: Ukee Days
description: Ucluelet's homegrown community festival — three days of local music, food, craft vendors, and small-town west coast fun. Friday festivities at the Village Green, Saturday and Sunday at the Fairgrounds. The kind of community event where locals outnumber tourists and everyone knows the band. A window into real life on the outer coast.

name: Winterlights at Tofino Botanical Gardens
description: A month-long celebration of light and art in the Tofino Botanical Gardens, running nightly through the darkest weeks of the year. Light installations, live entertainment, children's activities, and warm beverages at Darwin's Cafe. Car-free event with a free shuttle from the Village Green. Your pass is valid for the entire run — come back as many times as you like.

timing.approximate_dates: Late November through early January, nightly 5–9pm

name: Tofino Saturday Market
description: Every Saturday from Victoria Day weekend through Thanksgiving, 30+ local artisans, artists, and bakers set up on the Village Green. More creators' market than produce stand — reflecting Tofino's artisan culture. Live music under the gazebo, a playground for kids, and you can cycle there or take the free summer shuttle. The market captures Tofino's creative, community spirit.

timing.approximate_dates: Saturdays, Victoria Day weekend (May) through Thanksgiving (October)

name: Gray Whale Spring Migration
description: Beyond the festival, the gray whale migration itself is a major natural event — around 20,000 whales pass through the waters off Tofino and Ucluelet from March through May. Some resident grays stay through summer to feed in local waters. Whale watching tours run from both towns, and whales are regularly visible from shore at Wickaninnish Beach and the Wild Pacific Trail.

timing.approximate_dates: March through May; peak mid-March to mid-April

location: Waters off Tofino and Ucluelet; shore viewing from Wickaninnish Beach, Wild Pacific Trail

name: Dark Sky Viewing Season
description: Tofino and Ucluelet benefit from minimal light pollution and their remote position on Vancouver Island's outer coast. On clear nights the Milky Way is visible from the beach, and aurora borealis sightings are possible in winter. Crisp winter nights offer the best conditions. Grab a headlamp and walk out to Cox Bay or Long Beach for a natural light show backed by the sound of surf.

timing.approximate_dates: Best October through March on clear nights; aurora possible in winter

location: Cox Bay, Long Beach, Chesterman Beach, Pacific Rim National Park Reserve
